attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman is scheduled for laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She has no significant medical history aside from cholelithiasis but reports severe post-operative nausea and vomiting following two previous operations. She does not take any regular medications, is a non-smoker and social drinker. On examination pre-operatively, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her airway assessment is unremarkable with a Mallampati score of two.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate agent for maintenance of anaesthesia in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has multiple risk factors for post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV), including female sex, non-smoking status, and a strong history of PONV. The choice of anaesthetic maintenance agent can significantly impact the risk of PONV.

Propofol total intravenous anaesthesia (TIVA) is the most appropriate choice as it has antiemetic properties and is associated with a lower incidence of PONV compared to volatile agents. The antiemetic effect persists even after the drug is discontinued, making it particularly valuable in high-risk patients.

Desflurane is a volatile anaesthetic agent that, like other inhalational agents, is associated with a higher risk of PONV compared to propofol.

Sevoflurane is also a volatile agent that carries a significant risk of PONV. While it is a common choice of maintenance agent, it would not be the best choice for this patient given her risk factors.

Ketamine, while useful as an adjunct analgesic agent, can cause emergence phenomena and increase the risk of PONV when used as the primary maintenance agent. It would not be an appropriate choice for maintenance of anaesthesia in this case.

Nitrous oxide significantly increases the risk of PONV and should be avoided in high-risk patients.

Additional strategies to reduce PONV in this high-risk patient should include multimodal prophylactic antiemetics, adequate hydration, and minimising post-operative opioids where possible.
learningPoints: Total intravenous anaesthesia with propofol reduces post-operative nausea and vomiting compared to volatile agents.
Risk factors for PONV include female sex, non-smoking status, history of PONV/motion sickness, and use of post-operative opioids.
A multimodal approach to PONV prophylaxis is recommended in high-risk patients.
references: Jin Z, Gan TJ, Bergese SD. Prevention and Treatment of Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting (PONV): A Review of Current Recommendations and Emerging Therapies. Ther Clin Risk Manag. 2020 Dec 31;16:1305-1317. doi: 10.2147/TCRM.S256234. PMID: 33408475; PMCID: PMC7780848.

Gan TJ, Belani KG, Bergese S, Chung F, Diemunsch P, Habib AS, Jin Z, Kovac AL, Meyer TA, Urman RD, Apfel CC, Ayad S, Beagley L, Candiotti K, Englesakis M, Hedrick TL, Kranke P, Lee S, Lipman D, Minkowitz HS, Morton J, Philip BK. Fourth Consensus Guidelines for the Management of Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting. Anesth Analg. 2020 Aug;131(2):411-448. doi: 10.1213/ANE.0000000000004833. Erratum in: Anesth Analg. 2020 Nov;131(5):e241. doi: 10.1213/ANE.0000000000005245. PMID: 32467512.
